title: Making biochar in a steel drum retort
subtitle: Converting garden wood waste into stable carbon for soil amendment
excerpt: Biochar is wood pyrolysed at 400 to 700 °C in a low-oxygen environment. Unlike compost, the carbon in biochar does not continue to break down: it persists in soil for hundreds to thousands of years, sequestering carbon. A steel drum retort converts a 60 litre drum of dry wood off-cuts or prunings into 10 to 15 litres of finished biochar in two to three hours.
sourceNotes: International Biochar Initiative application guidelines 2023. UKBRC (UK Biochar Research Centre) guidance on small-scale production. DEFRA guidance on outdoor burning and smoke exemptions. Soil Association guidance on biochar soil amendment rates.

glossaryTerms.definition: Charcoal produced by pyrolysis of organic material at 400 to 700 °C in a limited-oxygen environment for use as a soil amendment. Distinct from charcoal for grilling: biochar is produced at controlled temperatures specifically for its stable porous carbon structure.

Thermal decomposition of organic material at elevated temperatures in the absence or near-absence of oxygen. Produces three products: biochar (solid), bio-oil (liquid condensate), and syngas (combustible gas). A retort captures and burns the syngas to sustain the pyrolysis temperature.

The long-term storage of carbon in a form that prevents its release as CO2. Biochar sequesters roughly 60 to 80 percent of the carbon in the original biomass as stable aromatic carbon structures, compared to composting which returns most carbon to the atmosphere as CO2 during decomposition.

A measure of the soil's ability to hold positively charged nutrient ions (calcium, magnesium, potassium) against leaching. The porous structure of biochar increases soil CEC, improving nutrient retention in sandy or low-organic soils.

content.text: Biochar

applied at 0.5 to 1 kg/m2 improves water retention in sandy soils, raises pH in acid soils, and helps the soil hold nutrients. It also locks carbon from wood waste into a stable form that stays in the soil for centuries. A drum retort makes this possible in a domestic garden.
cation exchange capacity
in depleted soils. The
carbon sequestration

aspect means that the carbon in wood waste that would otherwise rot (releasing CO2 over years) is instead locked into a stable form that persists in the soil for centuries. A drum retort makes this achievable in a domestic garden without specialist equipment.

Biochar production involves sustained high-temperature combustion. Site the retort on bare soil or concrete at least 5 m from any structure, fencing, or dry vegetation. Have a bucket of water or a hosepipe ready. Check local authority bylaws: in a Smoke Control Area the fire must use an exempt device or approved fuel. Do not operate during high wind conditions. Never leave the retort unattended while burning.
Building a simple drum retort

Obtain a 60 litre steel drum (an oil drum or similar) and a lid that fits. Drill 12 holes of 10 mm diameter around the base of the drum in two rows, 50 mm from the bottom. These supply primary air to the initial fire.
Drill 6 holes of 10 mm diameter around the drum at 200 mm from the top. These allow the combustible syngas produced by
pyrolysis
to exit and burn as a flame above the drum, sustaining the process without adding external fuel after the initial lighting.

Cut a 50 mm notch in the bottom rim of the drum to allow the base air holes to remain unobstructed when the drum rests on bricks or a steel grate. The drum needs to sit 50 to 75 mm above the ground on supports.
Running the retort

Dry the feedstock thoroughly before use. Wood with moisture content above 25 percent produces more smoke and less char. Air-dry prunings and off-cuts for at least two weeks before use, or dry in a greenhouse or polytunnel.

Light a small kindling fire at the top of a full drum of wood pieces (10 to 20 cm lengths, maximum 50 mm diameter). The fire is lit from the top and burns downward through the fuel. This top-lit updraft (TLUD) approach produces the least smoke during the early phase.

Once the flame at the upper holes burns blue or clear (showing syngas combustion), fit the lid loosely to limit oxygen and stop the char burning fully. Watch the retort throughout. White smoke early on means moisture burning off. A blue flame at the upper holes means pyrolysis is running.

When smoke stops and the flames at the upper holes die down, the pyrolysis is complete. Seal the drum completely with the lid and block the air holes with damp clay or wet rags to extinguish any remaining combustion. Allow to cool for at least three hours before opening.
Using the biochar

Charge the biochar before applying to soil: mix it with compost, manure, or liquid fertiliser at a ratio of one part biochar to three parts compost by volume and leave for two weeks. Uncharged biochar applied directly to soil initially locks up soluble nutrients rather than releasing them. After charging, apply at 250 to 500 g/m2 to planting beds and fork in to 200 mm depth with a garden fork.
What done well looks like

The drum burns for 90 minutes with a visible blue flame at the upper holes, indicating clean pyrolysis combustion. When the drum is opened after cooling, the contents are black, brittle, and uniform. The char rings hollow when tapped and is not grey (under-pyrolysed) or white (over-burned to ash). Sandy beds amended with charged biochar hold moisture noticeably better in dry spells within the first growing season.
What done badly looks like

Wet wood produced dense white smoke throughout the burn and the retort was abandoned after 20 minutes. The contents when cool are partially charred with unburned wood cores surrounded by grey ash where air got in around the loose lid. Uncharged raw biochar was applied directly to seedling beds and the seedlings showed nutrient deficiency symptoms within two weeks because the biochar adsorbed available nitrogen from the soil.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Dense smoke throughout the burn, not clearing to a blue flame
attrs.cause: Feedstock is too wet or the pieces are too large to fully pyrolyse
attrs.fix: Stop the burn, spread the feedstock to air-dry for a further week. Break pieces down to a maximum of 40 mm diameter before reloading. Moisture content above 20 percent produces smoke-producing smouldering rather than clean pyrolysis.

attrs.symptom: Char is grey and powdery when the drum is opened
attrs.cause: The lid was not sealed after pyrolysis was complete, allowing air to continue burning the char to ash
attrs.fix: Seal the drum immediately when the upper vent flames die down. Have the lid, clay, or sealing rags ready before the burn ends. Grey ash has lost most of its carbon structure and is not useful as biochar.

attrs.symptom: Plants in beds show yellowing leaves two weeks after raw biochar application
attrs.cause: Uncharged biochar is adsorbing available nitrogen from the soil
attrs.fix: Water the affected beds with a dilute liquid seaweed or fish emulsion feed to replace the adsorbed nitrogen. Going forward, always charge biochar in a compost mix for at least two weeks before applying to planted beds.
